subject: Re: rotated tiff
body:
Hi Tat,

the transpose operation results in the X and Y axes being swapped. This 
can't be replicated by an in-plane rotation. Here is an example:

1 2
3 4
5 6

becomes

1 3 5
2 4 6

The order of these operations in Warp is: flip X, flip Y, transpose. The 
best way to verify is to disable alignment and just produce a movie 
average. If you don't see any traces of the gain pattern there, the 
reference is oriented correctly.

Cheers,
Dimitry

On Thursday, November 22, 2018 at 5:17:40 PM UTC+1, TC wrote:
>
> Hi Dimitry,
>
> Probably a dumb question, but in order to make sure, what is the direction 
> used in the Transpose of the gain, clockwise or counter? 
>
> Practically, we have a user that has the tifs collected from SerialEM 
> rotated 90 degree clockwise, and it seems to us Transpose + flipY is better 
> than Transpose +flipX. What's your opinion on this?
> And the program FlipTiff.exe is using a lot of memory (~60Gb) for just 2 
> files in parallel.
>
> Thanks,
>
> Tat
>
